title: Kensington Square

infobox.value: is a [garden square](/wiki/Garden_square "Garden square") in [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington "Kensington"), London, [W8](/wiki/W_postcode_area "W postcode area"). It was built from 1692 on land acquired for the purpose in 1685 and is the oldest such square in Kensington. The houses facing, Nos. 1–45, are listed [Grade II](/wiki/Listed_building#Categories_of_listed_building "Listed building") for their architectural/historic merit.[[2]](#cite_note-LGO-2)
["Kensington Square and environs: Introduction"](http://www.british-history.ac.uk/survey-london/vol42/pp5-11). *British History Online (BHO)*. Retrieved 18 February 2016.

["Heythrop College: About us"](https://web.archive.org/web/20100314062126/http://www.heythrop.ac.uk/about-us). Archived from [the original](http://www.heythrop.ac.uk/about-us/) on 14 March 2010. Retrieved 9 April 2010.

[Heythrop College: How to find us](http://www.heythrop.ac.uk/about-us/how-to-find-us.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20100223052341/http://www.heythrop.ac.uk/about-us/how-to-find-us.html) 2010-02-23 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")

Garden square in Kensington, London, England

Kensington Square

**Kensington Square** is a [garden square](/wiki/Garden_square "Garden square") in [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington "Kensington"), London, [W8](/wiki/W_postcode_area "W postcode area"). It was built from 1692 on land acquired for the purpose in 1685 and is the oldest such square in Kensington. The houses facing, Nos. 1–45, are listed [Grade II](/wiki/Listed_building#Categories_of_listed_building "Listed building") for their architectural/historic merit.[[2]](#cite_note-LGO-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kensington_Square&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

In 1685, [Thomas Young](/wiki/Thomas_Young_(developer) "Thomas Young (developer)"), a woodcarver, acquired land in Kensington which he sought to develop, and as he later described it in 1701, "did sett out and appoint a considerable part thereof to be built into a large Square of large and substantial Houses fit for the Habitacion of persons of good Worth and Quality, with Courts and Yards before and Gardens lying backwards".[[3]](#cite_note-BHO-3)

In London, St. James's Square, Soho Square and Golden Square are a few years older, but in contrast with these Kensington Square still retains its residential character.

## Garden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kensington_Square&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Garden")]

The communal gardens were laid out in 1698 and are 0.36 hectares (0.89 acres) in size. The garden is private and not open to the public, though it has taken part in the annual [Open Garden Squares Weekend](/w/index.php?title=Open_Garden_Squares_Weekend&action=edit&redlink=1 "Open Garden Squares Weekend (page does not exist)").[[2]](#cite_note-LGO-2)

## Heythrop College

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kensington_Square&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Heythrop College")]

No. 23 was home to [Heythrop College](/wiki/Heythrop_College "Heythrop College"), [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London "University of London"), the specialist [Philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") and [Theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology") College of the University of London, until 2018. The College housed a library originally established in 1614 in [Louvain (Leuven)](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven") by the [Society of Jesus](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") (the Jesuits) for those studies.[[4]](#cite_note-4)
[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Former residents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kensington_Square&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Former residents")]

[Blue plaque](/wiki/Blue_plaque "Blue plaque") holders

The square includes the former homes of:

* composer [Hubert Parry](/wiki/Hubert_Parry "Hubert Parry") at No. 17
* liberal philosopher [John Stuart Mill](/wiki/John_Stuart_Mill "John Stuart Mill") at No. 18
* sanitary reformer and pathologist [John Simon](/wiki/John_Simon_(sanitary_reformer_and_pathologist) "John Simon (sanitary reformer and pathologist)") at No. 40;
* [Pre-Raphaelite](/wiki/Pre-Raphaelite_Brotherhood "Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood") artist [Edward Burne-Jones](/wiki/Edward_Burne-Jones "Edward Burne-Jones") at No. 41 —

Other homes belonged to, or were rented as their family home by:

* Lawyer and positivist Vernon Lushington at No. 36. He introduced one of the foremost Pre-Raphaelites, Edward Burne-Jones, to another, Dante Gabriel Rossetti, at the Working Men's College. The Lushingtons and Parrys frequently visited each other.
* Scholar and philanthropist [Richard Buckley Litchfield](/wiki/Richard_Buckley_Litchfield "Richard Buckley Litchfield") (1832–1903) at No. 31 with his wife
* [Henrietta Litchfield](/wiki/Henrietta_Litchfield "Henrietta Litchfield") (1843–1927), who was [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin")'s daughter.
* Their niece, artist [Gwen Raverat](/wiki/Gwen_Raverat "Gwen Raverat"), describes visits there in her memoir *[Period Piece](/wiki/Period_Piece "Period Piece")*.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Between 1831 and 1896 (the) [Kensington School](/wiki/Kensington_School "Kensington School") occupied two sites: No. 31, then No.s 25–29. It is notable as one of the founders of [the Football Association](/wiki/The_Football_Association "The Football Association") in 1863. The school built classrooms and fives courts in the gardens of the houses; all that remains is No. 27a, the cottage or small house behind No. 28.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kensington_Square&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

In the 2016 film *[The Exception](/wiki/The_Exception "The Exception")*, protagonist Mieke de Jong coyly inscribes a copy of landmark philosophical work *[Beyond Good and Evil](/wiki/Beyond_Good_and_Evil "Beyond Good and Evil")* with:

> For my ignorant friend.
> 15 Kensington square, London W.
